coredata.dc:title: Efficient and sustainable metal-free GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructrues for versatile visible-light-driven photoredox applications: Toward synergistic interaction of carbon materials
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Fuwei
Wen, Qingjuan
Hong, Mingzhu
Zhuang, Zanyong
Yu, Yan
coredata.dc:description:
A sheet-on-sheet and metal-free graphene/C3N4/carbon dots (GR/C3N4/CDots) ternary heterostructure with efficient visible-light photocatalysis for pollutants degradation has been reported. Via a convenient and hydrothermal method, large-sized GR nanosheets can serve as substrate for monodispersed dispersion of C3N4 nanosheets. A subsequent loading of very small amount of CDots (1wt%, 2–5nm) onto sheet-on-sheet heterostructure significantly enhance visible-light-driven photoredox performances of catalysts, including oxidation of organics pollutant (RhB, 96.0%) and reduction of heavy metals [Cr(VI), 83.6%]. Excellent catalytic properties of GR/C3N4/CDots heterostructure can be predominantly ascribed to the intimate interfacial contact among constructing ingredients, increased specific surface area and enhanced light adsorption derived from GR and CDots carbon materials. Particularly, the synergistic sheet-on-sheet interaction between C3N4 and carbon materials (GR and CDots) remarkably accelerate the electrons/holes separation, giving rise to significantly enhanced photoredox performances of GR/C3N4/CDots in comparison with other counterparts. The synergism effect between organic contaminants and heavy metals also contributed to the efficient performance of GR/C3N4/CDots, wherein the
O2− and
OH were the main active species in the photoredox reactions. This metals-free heterostructure shows favorable photostability in cyclic experiments. We anticipate that our work could provide a classical paradigm for rational design of efficient and visible-light-driven photocatalysts for their sustainable applications in wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-free
Photocatalysis
C3N4
Graphene

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51672046, 51472050 and 51402295).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustrating the synthetic route for C-CN-GR.

SEM images of a) B-CN; b) L-CN; c) CN-GR, the inset in plane c shows the SEM image of a GR nanosheet; d) SEM image of CN-GR; and e) C-CN-GR; f) EDS spectra and TEM images of g) C-CN-GR; h) Low-magnified TEM image of CDots on C3N4 matrix in ternary composite; i) HRTEM image of a single CDot attached to one surface of L-CN/GR; j–l) the corresponding elemental mapping images of C, N and O in the C-CN-GR sample, which is shown on larger scale in the inset of plane j.
a) XRD pattern and b) FTIR spectra of B-CN, L-CN, CN-GR, and C-CN-GR; c–d) XPS spectra of C-CN-GR.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of a) B-CN, b) L-CN, c) CN-GR, and d) C-CN-GR.
a) UV–vis absorption spectra and the (ahν)2 versus hν curve of B-CN, L-CN, CN-GR and C-CN-GR; and b) the corresponding photoluminescence spectra with an excitation wavelength of 325nm.

Photocatalytic activities of different samples toward degradation of a) RhB and b) reduction of Cr(VI); kinetic fitting of c) RhB and d) Cr(VI) (under room temperature and ambient pressure with visible light irradiation 800> λ >400nm).

a–b) HPLC chromatograms and TOC curve of intermediate product in aqueous samples treated by C-CN-GR under light irradiation for 0, 5, 30, and 120min; the inset in plane b shows the Ca(OH)2 solution before and after pumping of gas produced in photocatalytic system of C-CN-GR; c) XPS spectra (Cr 2p) of Cr on C-CN-GR.

a–b) Effect of initial Cr(VI) (or RhB) concentration on the degradation of RhB (or Cr) (the initial concentration of RhB (or Cr) was 10mg/L, illumination time: 30min); c) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of C-CN-GR methanol dispersion for DMPO-
O2 − and d) aqueous dispersion for DMPO-
OH; e) Photocatalytic performances of C-CN-GR in the presence of several scavengers (The dosage of scavengers: 0.1mmol/L, illumination time: 120min); f) The photocatalytic degradation of RhB and Cr(VI) over C-CN-GR over six cycles.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism of GR/C3N4/CDots ternary nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Efficient and sustainable metal-free GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructrues for versatile visible-light-driven photoredox applications: Toward synergistic interaction of carbon materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Materials Advanced Technology (Fuzhou University), Fujian Province University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Materials Advanced Technology (Fuzhou University)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, New Campus, Minhou, Fujian Province 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, New Campus, Minhou, Fujian Province 350108, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A sheet-on-sheet and metal-free graphene/C3N4/carbon dots (GR/C3N4/CDots) ternary heterostructure with efficient visible-light photocatalysis for pollutants degradation has been reported. Via a convenient and hydrothermal method, large-sized GR nanosheets can serve as substrate for monodispersed dispersion of C3N4 nanosheets. A subsequent loading of very small amount of CDots (1wt%, 2–5nm) onto sheet-on-sheet heterostructure significantly enhance visible-light-driven photoredox performances of catalysts, including oxidation of organics pollutant (RhB, 96.0%) and reduction of heavy metals [Cr(VI), 83.6%]. Excellent catalytic properties of GR/C3N4/CDots heterostructure can be predominantly ascribed to the intimate interfacial contact among constructing ingredients, increased specific surface area and enhanced light adsorption derived from GR and CDots carbon materials. Particularly, the synergistic sheet-on-sheet interaction between C3N4 and carbon materials (GR and CDots) remarkably accelerate the electrons/holes separation, giving rise to significantly enhanced photoredox performances of GR/C3N4/CDots in comparison with other counterparts. The synergism effect between organic contaminants and heavy metals also contributed to the efficient performance of GR/C3N4/CDots, wherein the
O2− and
OH were the main active species in the photoredox reactions. This metals-free heterostructure shows favorable photostability in cyclic experiments. We anticipate that our work could provide a classical paradigm for rational design of efficient and visible-light-driven photocatalysts for their sustainable applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis using semiconductor has been recognized as a promising technology for wide-spread solar energy conversion and environmental remediation [1,2]. In the past few decades, many research works on photocatalysis are mainly focused on the exploration of metals-oxide-based semiconductors, owing to their high thermal and chemical stability [3]. Nevertheless, the wide bandgap and low quantum efficiency of some metal-oxides-based semiconductors remarkably limit their potential applications as visible-light-responsive photocatalysts. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop non-metal oxide photocatalysts for a wide range of prospective applications for visible-light photocatalysis [4].

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymeric photocatalyst, has emerged as a new class of photocatalysis materials due to its relatively low band gap energy Eg of 2.7eV, and high valence band and conduction band positions [5,6]. However, it should be emphasized that photocatalytic performances of g-C3N4 usually suffers from high recombination probability of photogenerated electrons and holes, and the low utilization efficiency of visible light which accounts for the major part of the solar spectrum [7,8]. Construction of heterojunctions by combing C3N4 with rare metals, metals oxide or sulfides has been determined to be a common and efficient pathway to improve the photocatalytic performances of C3N4 [9,10]. Very recently, non-metal materials with the merits of non-toxicity and good stability have occasionally shown an ability to improve the visible-light response of C3N4 [11]. For instance, carbon nanodots (CDots) acting as electron reservoirs might suppress the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in CDots/C3N4 composite, thereby improving the hydrogen production efficiencies [12]. Similarly, in mimicking a natural photosystem, CDots serving as a preferred electron/energy transfer bridge contribute to efficient photodegradation of dyes [13].

Notwithstanding the progresses, the utilization of C3N4 is still limited by other disadvantages; specifically, C3N4 nanosheets acquired from ultrasonication may stack and congest during the loading of the cocatalysts or in the photocatalytic reactions [14]. To surmount this obstacle, graphene oxide (GO), as the precursor of graphene (GR), possessing unique layered structure and fascinating electronic, mechanical, and chemical properties might be a suitable substrate for evenly dispersing C3N4 and thus retards the agglomeration of C3N4 nanosheets [15]. Given the enhanced light absorption, and more importantly, excellent electron conductivity of carbon materials, it is rationally speculated that concurrent decoration of C3N4 with GR and CDots in an intimate integration fashion would significantly improve the photocatalytic performances of C3N4. It is worthy to mentioned that, the π–π∗ or charge-transfer interactions [16] among GR, C3N4, and CDots could promote the transport of photogenerated charge carries on C3N4. Up to date, rational construction of GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructure via a facile one-pot synthetic strategy in combination with systematic investigation on the photocatalytic mechanism have not yet been reported.

In this work, GR/C3N4/CDots ternary nanocomposites have been prepared by a facile hydrothermal approach by which of GR, C3N4 and CDots components were intimately integrated. It has been revealed that photocatalytic performances of C3N4 can be conspicuously improved by loading a small amount of GR and CDots toward both the degradation of organic dye pollutants (e.g. RhB) and reduction of heavy metals ions (e.g. Cr) under visible light irradiation. The synergistic integration of GR and CDots contribute to the significantly enhanced photoredox activities of GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructure. Moreover, the underlying photocatalytic mechanism was investigated. The regeneration and reusability of the ternary composite photocatalysts was also examined.

Natural graphite flakes (325mesh) and graphite rod (99.99%) were purchased from Alfa-Aesar. Concentrated sulfuric acid (98%, H2SO4), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (P.R. China). Alcohol, peroxide (30%, H2O2), ammonia, melamine and Rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corp. (P.R. China). All the reactants were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.0MΩcm−1) in the experiments was prepared by using an ultra-pure purification system.

GO was synthesized by a modified Hummers’ method [17]. First, the graphite flakes (5g) were added into a solution of H2SO4 (15mL, 98%), K2S2O8 (2.5g) and P2O5 (2.5g) at 80°C. The reaction was maintained for 6h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the product was washed with ultrapure water until pH=7, filtered and dried at 60°C overnight. In the second step, a beaker with 115mL condensed H2SO4 was placed into an ice bath. The pre-oxidized graphite and KMnO4 (15g) were added sequentially into the beaker slowly under stirring at a temperature below 20°C. The reaction was kept at 35°C for 3.5h and at 98°C for 15min. Then, ultrapure water and H2O2 (6.5mL, 30%) were added into the obtained solution to yield a golden yellow solution. Finally, the resultant product was washed with 115mL of HCl (1/10, v/v) solution and ultrapure water, and dried under vacuum at room temperature for 6h.

GR-like C3N4 2D nanosheets were prepared by a mixed-solvent exfoliation method according to the standard procedure [18]. First, the melamine was heated at 550°C for 3h in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 2.3°C/min. After cooling in the air, the yellow bulk g-C3N4 (B-CN) was ground into powder. Second, the bulk g-C3N4 powders (1g) was added to a mixed solvent (300mL) of ethanol and water (the volume ratio of alcohol and water was 3:4). Afterward, the suspension was ultrasonicated for 10h and then centrifuged at 2000rpm for 8min to remove the residual bulk C3N4 nanoparticles. Finally, the C3N4 layered nanosheets (L-CN) in the supernatant were collected by vacuum filtration, and dried at 60°C in a vacuum oven.

CDots were synthesized by using a standard electrochemical method [19]. Two graphite rods were inserted into the ultrapure water to serve as the anode and cathode. A 36 V DC was applied between the two electrodes. Under continuous stirring for 120h, a dark yellow solution was formed in the reaction cell. The dark yellow solution was dried in an oven. The as-obtained powder was weighted and added to ultrapure water to form a CDots aqueous solution with a concentration of 4mg/L. Then, an ammonia solution (30mL) was mixed with of the CDots solution (30mL, 4mg/L) under stirring to form a homogeneous solution. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactors which was heated at 150°C for 7h. A yellow supernatant was collected by centrifugation. Subsequently, the yellow solution was exposed in the air at room temperature to remove the excess ammonia until the pH reaches 7. Finally, the CDots solution was dried at 60°C and ground to a powder. The CDots were weighed and added to ultrapure water to form a CDots solution (2mg/L) for the following experiments.

GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructures (C-CN-GR) was fabricated via a hydrothermal method as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Sepcifically, GO (0.1g), C3N4 nanosheets (1g) powder and CDots aqueous solution (5mL, 2mg/L) were added into 300mL of an ethanol and water solution (the volume ratio of alcohol and water was 3:4). The suspension was ultrasonicated for 1h. The mixed solution was then transferred to four 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactors which was heated at 180°C for 3h. The GR/C3N4/CDots ternary nanocomposites were then collected by rotary evaporation at 60°C. The C3N4/GR (CN-GR) binary composite without CDots was prepared by a similar method.

The morphology of the samples was examined by SEM (Philips XL30) and TEM (FEI TECNAI G2F20). The crystal structure of the samples was determined with XRD (X’Pert Pro MPD, Philips, Holland) equipped with Cu Kα radiation under 36kV and 30mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of all of the catalysts were recorded using a TJ270-30A infrared spectrophotometer (Tianjin, China). UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) was performed on a Hitachi UV-3010 UV–vis spectrophotometer. XPS (PHI 5000 Versa Probe) was used for the surface analysis of the prepared ternary nanocomposite. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured with an ASAP2020 M apparatus (Micromeritics Instrument Corp.). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalysts were measured on the Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer with an excitation wavelength approximately 325nm. The N-deethylated intermediates were detected by High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) technique (Agilent 1260) equipped with UV–vis diode array detector using a C18 inversed-phase column. The total organic carbon (TOC) was measured by Shimadzu TOC-5000A. EPR spectra were recorded at room temperature using a Bruker A-300-EPR X-band spectrometer.

Photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts were evaluated by testing the degradation efficiencies of RhB and Cr(VI), using a Xe lamp (500W) equipped with a UV irradiation source filter (λ >420nm). In a standard photocatalytic experiment, the photocatalyst (30mg) was dispersed in a mixed aqueous solution of RhB (50mL, 20ppm) or K2Cr2O7 (50mL, 20ppm), and the suspension was stirred for 30min in the dark to ensure that it reaches the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, the suspension was irradiated by visible light for 120min. At specific time intervals, a small amount of the suspension (3.0mL) was withdrawn, and centrifuged to remove the particles. The concentration of RhB and Cr(VI) in the supernatant were analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometry. The degradation efficiency of the target pollutant was calculated using the Lambert-Beer Eq. (2-1), where C0 was the initial concentration; and Ct was the concentration during photocatalysis.
(2-1)
Degradation efficiency
( % ) = ( 1 -
C
t
/
C
0
) × 100 %

The morphology and chemical composition of the photocatalysts were first investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, B-CN has layered structure composed of tightly stacked C3N4 nanosheets with sizes on the order of hundreds of nanometers. By solvent-exfoliation treatment, L-CN monolayers was successfully exfoliated from B-CN (Fig. 2b). Subsequently, L-CN was hybridized with GR and CDots. As shown in Fig. 2c–e, both CN-GR binary and C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposites maintain a well-defined 2D sheet structure, with C3N4 nanosheets uniformly dispersing on the large GR substrate (Fig. 2c–d).

Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum in Fig. 2f was utilized to investigate the relative percentage of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) elements in C-CN-GR ternary composite. Generally, the stoichiometric N:C ratio of pure C3N4 is approximately 1.33. When 10wt% GR was incorporated onto the C3N4, in theory, the N:C ratio of C3N4 will decrease to 1.16. Consistently in our experiment, the atomic ratio of N:C in C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite was determined to be ca. 1.12. This result confirms the combination of CN with GR in C-CN-GR ternary heterostructure.

Elemental mapping and SEM image of C-CN-GR in Fig. 2j–l clear show that, C, N and O are well distributed over the C-CN-GR nanocomposite. In addition, Fig. 2g–i show that nanodots with a diameter of 2–5nm were evenly distributed on the L-CN nanosheet in C-CN-GR nanocomposite. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals that these nanodots have a clear fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.318nm, which corresponds well to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2i) reported in literature [20]. From these results it can be observed that CDots and L-CN nanosheets are well dispersed on the GR nanosheets, probably due to the structural compatibility of CDots, C3N4 nanosheets and GR [16].

The phase and structure of photocatalysts were further probed by XRD studies. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the two distinct diffraction peaks at 13.9° and 27.5° for B-CN are in good agreement with the reported GCN standard (JCPDS 87-1526) [4]. The two diffraction peaks are associated with 0.681 and 0.324nm interlayer spacing derived from the in-plane repeated units and the stacking of the conjugated aromatic systems in the layered structure of GCN, respectively [4,21]. After solvent-exfoliation treatment the intensities of the two peaks were remarkably decreased, indicating that L-CN was successfully exfoliated from B-CN. However, none of characteristic diffraction peaks of GO were observed in XRD pattern of CN-GR binary, or the C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite. It implies GO components in hybrid nanocomposites was completely reduced to GR after hydrothermal treatment [22]. Alternatively, there is also no difference observed between the CN-GR and C-CN-GR, likely due to low loading amount and the weak diffraction of CDots.

Fig. 3b presents the FT-IR spectra of B-CN, L-CN, CN-GR and C-CN-GR. In FT-IR spectrum of pure B-CN, the peaks at 1241, 1323, 1411, 1573, and 1644cm−1 correspond to the classical stretching modes of C–N heterocycles, and 806cm−1 was ascribed to breathing mode of triazine units [23]. It confirms the XRD analysis showing the formation of C3N4. L-CN, CN-GR and C-CN-GR have similar FT-IR spectra within 1100–1600cm−1 region [24]. This results indicate that pretreatment stage, involving the solvent-assisted exfoliation and subsequent loading of co-catalysts, does not destroy the lattice structure of C3N4.

XPS further explores the chemical composition of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3c–d, C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite is mainly composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) elements, which is in line with the EDS result. Specifically, high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into four peaks, among which the peak at 398.3eV corresponds to sp2 hybridized aromatic N bonded to carbon atoms (CNC), and the peak at 399.3eV respenting the tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N(C)3 or HN(C)2 [25]. In addition, the weaker one at 400.8eV is attributable to the quaternary N bonded to three carbon atoms in the aromatic cycles [19], while the peak centered at 405.7eV corresponds to the π excitations [26]. These peaks are accompanied with CN materials, confirming the formation of C3N4 [27]. In addition, high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) has the peak at 284.3eV attributed to sp2-hybridized carbons (CC), and the peaks at 284.9, 286.6, 287.8 and 291.2eV ascribed to COH, CNC, and CO bonds, respectively [21]. These results also confirms the hybridization of GR in the C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite.

Fig. 4 shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of B-CN, L-CN, CN-GR and C-CN-GR. All the samples exhibited a Type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with an H3 hysteresis loop in the relative pressure range of 0.6–1.0. The BET specific surface area of the L-CN is approximately 37.6m2/g which is larger than that of B-CN (28.8m2/g). Interestingly, the specific surface areas of CN-GR and C-CN-GR nanocomposites increased to about approximately 45.8 and 51.9m2/g, respectively. It suggests the introduction of GR and CDots is beneficial for boosting the specific surface area of nanocomposites, and as a result it might provide more reactive sites for the adsorption and catalysis of reactants.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the bandgap of C3N4 estimated using the Kubelka–Munk function is about 2.7eV, which enables the absorption of visible light at 461nm and coincides with the value reported in the literature [4]. Similarly, L-CN, CN-GR, C-CN-GR also demonstrate strong absorption in visible region. Apparently, visible-light harvesting capability of CN-GR was substantially enhanced with adsorption edge redshift to 511nm. The result strongly indicates that integration of GR in the composites could significantly promote the light harvesting efficiency of C3N4. Noteworthily, the addition of CDots in the CN-GR binary nanocomposites leads to a further increase in the absorption edge to around 551nm, which highlights the pivotal role of CDots for enhancing the light harvesting in visible region and thus enable the excitation of more electrons over ternary nanocomposites under visible light irradiation [28].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was utilized to probe the charge-carriers separation/recombination behavior of photocatalyst. In general, higher PL intensity means high recombination probability of charge carriers, while lower one indicates more efficient carriers participating in the photocatalytic process [29]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the main PL emission peaks at ca. 450nm for four representative samples confirms that the π-conjugated system of C3N4 was not destroyed by exfoliation and modification treatment [30]. The decreased PL intensity of CN-GR binary and C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposites in comparison with B-CN and L-CN (i.e., C-CN-GR<CN-GR<L-CN<B-CN) implies that recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes was inhibited with the introduction of GR and CDots. The reason for this may involve two factors: 1) The Fermi level of GR is located at −0.08eV vs NHE [31], and the conduction band edge of C3N4 is −1.17eV vs NHE [4]. As a result, the photogenerated electrons can transfer from the C3N4 to GR owing to favorable band alignment [16]. 2) It has been ascertained that CDots can also act as an electron reservoir to trap electrons emitted from C3N4 and thus further help to suppress the electron/hole recombination [32]. These two factors can contribute to decrease in the photoluminescence intensity, which improves the photocatalytic performance of C3N4.

Photocatalytic activities of samples were evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of RhB and reduction of K2Cr2O7. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the concentrations of RhB and K2Cr2O7 slightly decrease in dark condition for all samples. This indicates that the catalysts demonstrate weak adsorption toward reactants. Moreover, it is clear to see that CN-GR and C-CN-GR exhibit enhanced adsorption which is conducive to the catalytic reaction. Notably, all the samples reached the adsorption equilibrium state within 30min.

As shown in Fig. 6, in the absence of photocatalyst, no more than 3.0% of RhB and 2.4% of Cr(VI) was removed after 120min light irradiation. It shows that the redox of RhB and Cr(VI) cannot be realized under visible light irradiation without catalyst. As indicated by Testa et al. [33], the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is through successive one electron process, and the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(V) is the first step. Since the redox potential of Cr(VI)/Cr(V) (E 0 =0.6V vs. NHE) is more negative than RhB+ /RhB (1.2V vs. NHE) [34], the electron transfer from RhB to Cr(VI) is thermodynamically prohibited.

Only 42.1% of RhB and 34.6% of Cr(VI) were removed by B-CN after 60min irradiation, while 78.9% of RhB and 43.9% of Cr(VI) by L-CN. It means that exfoliated C3N4 nanosheets possess more active reaction centers relative to bulk counterpart. Although both B-CN and L-CN showed efficient removal ability toward RhB, they exhibited weak photocatalytic efficiency (<50%) for reduction of Cr(VI).

With the addition of GR and CDots, it is clearly seen that photocatalytic efficiency of C3N4 toward reduction of Cr(VI) within 60min was improved to 53.7% and 60.1%, respectively. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic efficiency of CN-GR and C-CN-GR toward RhB within 60min also rose to 93.5% and 96.0%, respectively. The rate constant of RhB degradation using CN-GR and C-CN-GR increased to k=0.030min−1 and k=0.048min−1, respectively. As irradiation time prolonged to 120min, the photocatalytic efficiency of C-CN-GR toward reduction of Cr(VI) reached to 83.6%, which is remarkably faster than the rate of B-CN, L-CN and CN-GR as showing in Fig. 6c–d.

To investigate the pathway by which RhB degraded, the products presenting in aqueous samples during photocatalytic process was determined by HPLC and TOC techniques. HPLC spectra in Fig. 7 a show that under light irradiation of RhB for 5min, four peaks attributable to N-deethylation intermediates were observed, including N,N-diethyl-N′-ethylrhodamine (DER), N-diethylrhodamine (DR), N-ethylrhodamine (ER), and rhodamine (R) as indicated by Chen et al. [35]. As the time prolonging over 30min, the peaks for N-deethylated intermediates almost disappeared, revealing further degradation of N-deethylation intermediates into simple organic compounds or CO2 [36].

TOC analysis (Fig. 7b) of aqueous samples obtained in RhB degradation system show that, the total organic carbon concentration decreased to 60% within 120min. Despite a slow decrease of total organic carbon concentration, it still confirms that the RhB can gradually be degraded into CO2. The generation of CO2 was verified by the phenomenon (the inset figure in Fig. 7b) that, the clear Ca(OH)2 solution became turbid after being pumped with the gas produced in photocatalytic system. These results suggest the transformation of RhB occurs following the Eq. (3-1). What should be noted is that, it seems the degradation of RhB into N-deethylation intermediates and simple organic molecules is a rapid process, while it requires more time to degrade these intermediates into CO2.
(3-1)
RhB
⟶
- Et
DER
⟶
- Et
DR
⟶
- Et
ER
⟶
- Et
R → simple molecules →
CO
2
+
H
2
O

XPS spectrum of Cr element on C-CN-GR was recorded to investigate whether or not the Cr(VI) was completely reduced to Cr(III). Due to the much small content of Cr in samples, a weak Cr 2p spectrum was obtained (Fig. 7c). It can be found that only peak of Cr(III) (E b =577.2eV) appeared in the high resolution of Cr 2p spectra, similar like what described in literature [37], and none of signals for Cr(VI) (E b ≈579eV) was detected. This result suggests most of the Cr(VI) were reduced to Cr(III).

In the following, the influence of the initial concentration of target pollutants on the photodegradation efficiency was investigated to study the underlying mechanism. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the photodegradation efficiency of RhB first increased with a rise in Cr(VI) concentration. However, it reached a plateau when the coexisting concentration of Cr(VI) was over 25mg/L. The reason for this can be explained in the following. As shown in Eq. (4-1), electrons were first excited from the conduction band (CB) to the valence band (VB) over C3N4 under visible light irradiation. Then, as indicated in Eq. (4-2), Cr(VI) could serve as an electron acceptor, promoting the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of C3N4. The higher Cr(VI) concentration means the photocatalyst can capture more photogenerated electrons for reduction reaction of Cr(VI), leaving more photogenerated holes to efficiently oxidize RhB and showing enhanced degradation efficiency of RhB [38]. However, photodegradation efficiency of RhB decrease under concentrated Cr(VI) solution (30mg/L), probably owing to that the adsorption of RhB will be inhibited in a concentrated Cr(VI) solution.

N

+ h ν →
e
-

h

( VB )
(4-2)

+ 14
H
+
+ 16
e
-
→ 2 Cr ( III ) + 7
H
2
O

Similarly, as shown in Fig. 8b, the presence of RhB could remarkably enhance the photoreduction rate of Cr(VI). The efficiency of C-CN-GR toward reduction of Cr(VI) in the presence of RhB was nearly three times higher than that in the absence of RhB. The role of RhB in the photocatalytic process can be reflected by Eqs. (4-3)–(4-5), in which RhB acts as an electron donor leading to an enhanced photoreduction efficiency of Cr(VI).
(4-3)

2
+

O
2 -

+ 2

2
(4-5)
H
2
O

+

-
→
OH +
OH
-
(4-6)
RhB +
OH →
e
-
+ simple molecules →
CO
2
+
H
2
O

To detect the in-situ formed active radicals in current reaction system, EPR with the spin trapping agent 5,5-dimethyl1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was performed. As shown in Fig. 8(c–d), no EPR signal was observed in the dark, and there is also none of EPR signal of DMPO-
OH adducts under visible and UV light irradiation. However, EPR signals for DMPO-
O2 − adducts was clearly observed under visible and UV light irradiation. It suggests that molecular O2 can efficiently scavenge photogenerated electrons to form superoxide radical (
O2 −) playing an important role in the photocatalytic process. To further probe the underlying mechanism of C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite, different scavengers for varied active species were added into reaction system. Specifically, isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), were used to capture the
O2 −,
OH, and h+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8e, photocatalytic performance of C-CN-GR toward degradation of RhB was clearly inhibited by the addition of IPA and BQ. These results indicate that
O2 − and
OH play critical roles in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. In addition, reduced degradation efficiency of RhB was accompanied with decreased degradation efficiency of Cr(VI), confirming synergistic effect of RhB and Cr(VI) during the photocatalytic process.

The photostability of C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 8f, photoactivities of C-CN-GR nanocomposite toward degradation of RhB was retained with high efficiency over six consecutive experiments. The efficiency of Cr(VI) solely slightly decreased by 8.5%. The result suggests that C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposites demonstrate favorable photostability.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of C-CN-GR ternary nanocomposite is illustrated in Fig. 9 . First of all, the large layered GR can serve as the mechanical support beneficial for dispersion of C3N4 nanosheets to form spatially 2D-2D hybrid heterostructure. Moreover, large specific surface area of GR/C3N4/CDots nanocomposite and its enhanced adsorption ability for the contaminants contribute to the photocatalytic process. Specifically, 1) simultaneous loading of CDots and GR promote the visible light absorption of C3N4, which enables photoexcitation of more electrons from the VB to the CB [28]. 2) The small CDots with many active sites can serve as an efficient electron reservoir to capture photogenerated electrons, thus hinder the recombination of electron-hole carriers [39]. Meanwhile, the GR component can also help to suppress the charge recombination by capturing photogenerated electrons owing to its excellent electron conductivity and mobility [40]. 3) Synergistic effect between photoreduction of Cr(VI) and oxidation of RhB also result in significantly enhanced photocatalytic performances of GR/C3N4/CDots nanocomposite. On the other hand, owing to the formation of hetero-junction among CDots, C3N4 and GR and their intimate interfacial interaction, photogenerated electrons can efficiently transfer to the CDots and GR nanosheets, showing enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated holes/electrons pairs. In this way, excited electrons participates in the reduction of Cr(VI) and the holes on the valence band of C3N4 (the valence band edge potential [4] of C3N4 was 1.53eV vs NHE) oxidize the RhB to CO2 and H2O.

In summary, renewable metal-free GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructure was synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, by which intimate combination of C3N4 with GR nanosheets and CDots was achieved. The result showed that GR nanosheet can not only serve as suitable substrate for uniform dispersion of C3N4 nanosheets, but also help to accelerate the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Moreover, it was found that a subsequent loading of small amount of CDots (1wt%, 2–5nm) leads to a substantially increase in photocatalytic performances of C3N4. Over 99.6% of RhB and 83.6% of Cr(VI) were removed by GR/C3N4/CDots under visible light irradiation for 120min. The GR/C3N4/CDots ternary nanocomposite demonstrates favorable photostability, which provides a very convenient chemical route to large scale modification of metal-free photocatalyst. The excellent photoactivity of GR/C3N4/CDots ternary heterostructure was derived from all ingredients. GR and CDots afford increased specific surface area and enhanced visible light absorption as well as the π–π∗ interactions among the carbon materials (GR, C3N4 and CDots), which substantially accelerates the separation of photogenerated electrons/holes. The
O2 − and
OH radicals were determined to be the active species. We anticipate that our work could shed some new highlights for rational synthesis of low-cost, stable and metal-free photocatalysis for sustainable wastewater purification. We believe that the GR/C3N4/CDots ternary nanocomposites could be applied to hydrolysis generate hydrogen, photoreduction of CO2 and energy storage/conversion, owing to the speedy separation of photogenerated electrons/holes. With the limitation in space, this article does not contain these, and these will be explored in further researches.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51672046, 51472050 and 51402295).
